section.title: A Lament over Pharaoh
section.sectionRationale: The oracles against Egypt reach their end with a double lament: Pharaoh, pictured first as the primeval sea monster whose thrashing churns the waters, is netted and cast down under a darkened sky; then in a second dirge he is consigned to Sheol to lie among the very nations, Assyria, Elam, Meshech and Tubal, Edom, that already fill the pit with the slain.
section.thesis: Egypt's dragon-like pride, which churned the nations like the primeval chaos monster, is undone by the LORD's sword in Babylon's hand, and Pharaoh finds his only 'comfort' in discovering he is not alone among the uncircumcised dead.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the first lament, picturing Pharaoh as the monster of the seas brought down under a darkened sky; the sword of Babylon and Egypt's emptied land; and the second lament, consigning Egypt to Sheol among the whole roll call of fallen, once-terrifying nations.

units.label: The monster of the seas, and the sword of Babylon
units.note: the first lament, a darkened sky, and Egypt's desolation

verses.text: In the twelfth year, on the first day of the twelfth month, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Son of man, take up a {{qinah|lament}} for Pharaoh king of Egypt and say to him: 
‘You are like a lion among the nations; you are like a {{tanniyn|monster}} in the seas. You thrash about in your rivers, churning up the waters with your feet 
and muddying the streams.’

This is what the Lord GOD says: 
‘I will spread My net over you with a company of many peoples, 
and they will draw you up in My net.
I will abandon you on the land and hurl you into the open field. I will cause all the birds of the air to settle upon you, and all the beasts of the earth to eat their fill of you.
I will put your flesh on the mountains and fill the valleys with your remains.
I will drench the land with the flow of your blood, all the way to the mountains, the ravines will be filled.

When I extinguish you, I will cover the heavens 
and darken their stars. I will cover the sun with a cloud, and the moon will not give its light.
All the shining lights in the heavens I will darken over you, and I will bring darkness upon your land,’ declares the Lord GOD.

‘I will trouble the hearts of many peoples, when I bring about your destruction among the nations, 
in countries you do not know.

I will cause many peoples to be appalled over you, and their kings will shudder in horror because of you when I brandish My sword before them. On the day of your downfall each of them will tremble 
every moment for his life.’

For this is what the Lord GOD says: 
‘The sword of the king of Babylon will come against you!

I will make your hordes fall by the swords of the mighty, 
the most ruthless of all nations. They will ravage the pride of Egypt and all her multitudes will be destroyed.
I will slaughter all her cattle beside the abundant waters. No human foot will muddy them again, and no cattle hooves will disturb them.
Then I will let her waters settle and will make her rivers flow like oil,’ declares the Lord GOD.
‘When I make the land of Egypt a desolation and empty it of all that filled it, when I strike down all who live there, then they will know that I am the LORD.’
This is the lament they will chant for her; the daughters of the nations will chant it. Over Egypt and all her multitudes they will chant it, declares the Lord GOD.”
In the twelfth year, on the fifteenth day of the month, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, wail for the multitudes of Egypt, and consign her and the daughters of the mighty nations to the depths of the earth with those who descend to the Pit:
Whom do you surpass in beauty? Go down and be placed with the {{arel|uncircumcised}}!
They will fall among those slain by the sword. The sword is appointed! Let them drag her away along with all her multitudes.
Mighty chiefs will speak from the midst of Sheol about Egypt and her allies: ‘They have come down and lie with the uncircumcised, with those slain by the sword.’

units.label: Among the fallen nations in Sheol
units.note: Assyria, Elam, Meshech, Tubal, Edom, and the terror-spreading nations, all already there

verses.text: Assyria is there with her whole company; her graves are all around her. All of them are slain, fallen by the sword.
Her graves are set in the depths of the Pit, and her company is all around her grave. All of them are slain, fallen by the sword, those who once spread terror in the land of the living.

Elam is there with all her multitudes around her grave. All of them are slain, fallen by the sword, those who went down uncircumcised to the earth below, who once spread their terror in the land of the living. They bear their disgrace with those who descend to the Pit.

Among the slain they prepare a resting place for Elam with all her hordes, with her graves all around her. All of them are uncircumcised, slain by the sword, although their terror was once spread in the land of the living. They bear their disgrace with those who descend to the Pit. 
They are placed among the slain.

Meshech and Tubal are there with all their multitudes, 
with their graves all around them. All of them are uncircumcised, slain by the sword, because they spread their terror in the land of the living.

They do not lie down with the fallen warriors of old, who went down to Sheol with their weapons of war, whose swords were placed under their heads, whose shields rested on their bones, although the terror of the mighty was once in the land of the living.

But you too will be shattered and lie down among the uncircumcised, 
with those slain by the sword.
Edom is there, and all her kings and princes, who despite their might are laid among those slain by the sword. They lie down with the uncircumcised, with those who descend to the Pit.

All the leaders of the north and all the Sidonians are there; they went down in disgrace with the slain, despite the terror of their might. They lie uncircumcised with those slain by the sword and bear their shame with those who descend to the Pit.
Pharaoh will see them and be comforted over all his multitude, Pharaoh and all his army, slain by the sword, declares the Lord GOD.

For I will spread My terror in the land of the living, so that Pharaoh and all his multitude will be laid to rest among the uncircumcised, 
with those slain by the sword, declares the Lord GOD.”

qinah.gloss: A lament or dirge, the formal genre of funeral poetry sung over the dead. Ezekiel is told to sing this over Egypt while Pharaoh still lives, as if he were already dead.

tanniyn.gloss: A sea monster or dragon, elsewhere naming the primeval chaos-creature Rahab or Leviathan (Isaiah 51:9, Psalm 74:13-14). Casting Pharaoh as this creature makes his pride cosmic in scope, a rival to God's ordered world.

arel.gloss: Uncircumcised, a term of covenant disgrace in Israel's vocabulary. Applying it to Egypt and the other fallen nations groups their proud, terror-spreading might into one shared shame in death.

basic.context: This closes the oracles against Egypt with two funeral laments sung over Pharaoh while he is still alive. First he is pictured as a great monster of the seas, thrashing and muddying the waters, netted and hurled onto open ground under a darkened sky. Then, in a second dirge, Egypt is consigned to Sheol to lie among the whole roll call of once-feared nations, Assyria, Elam, Meshech and Tubal, Edom, already there.
basic.hermeneutics: These are real prophetic funeral songs, using the ancient qinah, lament, form and vivid mythic imagery (the chaos monster, the darkened heavens) to strip Egypt's pride down to size before it has even fallen. The point is not a literal zoological description but a declaration that no earthly power, however monstrous or terrifying, escapes God's sword.
basic.originalLanguages: Ezekiel is told to take up a qinah, a formal funeral lament, over a king who is still alive. Pharaoh is called a tanniyn, a sea monster or dragon, the same word Scripture uses elsewhere for the primeval chaos-creature Rahab or Leviathan, casting Egypt's pride as opposition to God's ordered world itself. And every fallen nation shares the disgrace of being arel, uncircumcised, in death.
basic.typology: The chaos-monster imagery used for Pharaoh echoes the same imagery Scripture later applies to the ancient serpent, the devil and Satan, Revelation 12:9, 20:2. Just as God nets and slays the monster Pharaoh here, so Christ decisively defeats the ancient dragon at the cross, Colossians 2:15, and will finally cast him into the lake of fire, Revelation 20:10.
basic.christ: The darkened sun, moon, and stars over Egypt's judgment, verses 7-8, echo the plague of darkness at the Exodus, Exodus 10:21-23, and anticipate the darkness over Calvary, Matthew 27:45, when God's judgment on sin fell on His own Son rather than on the nations who deserved it. And the chaos-dragon defeated here previews the ancient serpent's final undoing through Christ's cross and return, Revelation 12:9, 20:2, 10.
basic.god: God alone commands the depths, the stars, and the nations; He nets the great sea monster as easily as a fisherman his catch, showing that no power, however primeval or terrifying in its pride, escapes His sovereign judgment. Yet He is also the God who lets Pharaoh find his only 'comfort' in shared disgrace, a solemn, sobering justice rather than cruelty for its own sake.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 51:9-10 calls on the LORD's arm to awake as it did against Rahab and the dragon, the same chaos-monster imagery used of Egypt here. Exodus 10:21-23 records the plague of darkness over Egypt at the Exodus. Revelation 12:9 and 20:2 identify the ancient dragon as the devil and Satan, finally defeated. Colossians 2:15 shows Christ disarming every proud, monstrous power at the cross.
basic.oneStory: This is the last word against Egypt's pride: pictured as the primeval sea monster, Pharaoh is netted, his skies darkened, and his multitude consigned to Sheol among every other terror-spreading nation already there. It anticipates the far greater and final undoing of the ancient dragon himself, accomplished at the cross and consummated when he is cast forever into the lake of fire.

context.literary: Two dated oracles, each a formal funeral lament (qinah) sung over Egypt while Pharaoh is still alive, the last two entries in the extended sequence of oracles against Egypt (chapters 29-32).
context.historical: Delivered within a fortnight of the previous oracle, in the twelfth year of the exile, shortly before news of Jerusalem's fall reached the exiles; these final laments over Egypt strip away the last hope many exiles held that Egypt might rescue Judah from Babylon.
context.canonical: Draws on Scripture's wider chaos-monster imagery for God's enemies, Rahab and Leviathan in Isaiah and the Psalms, which the New Testament identifies fully as the ancient serpent, the devil, finally defeated in Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To sing Egypt's funeral song before its fall, stripping Pharaoh's proud, monstrous self-image down to size, and to show that Egypt's coming ruin joins it to a long line of once-terrifying nations already lying disgraced in Sheol.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These are dated, historical funeral laments over a specific ancient kingdom; they are not prescribing a ritual. Their enduring, prescriptive truth is that no power, however great or monstrous its pride, stands beyond God's sword or outside the coming reckoning of every nation before Him.

debates.question: Is the 'monster' (tannin) of verse 2 simply Egypt's well-known Nile crocodile, or does it carry the fuller mythic freight of the chaos-dragon found elsewhere in Scripture?

views.label: A literal crocodile image
views.case: Many note Egypt was famous for the Nile crocodile, and Pharaoh's earlier boast, 'my Nile is my own, I made it', Ezekiel 29:3, fits a proud crocodile-king image without needing wider mythic background.

views.label: The chaos-dragon of biblical imagery
views.case: Others point out the same Hebrew word describes Rahab or Leviathan elsewhere as a symbol of chaos opposed to God's ordered creation, Isaiah 51:9, Psalm 74:13-14, so Pharaoh here is cast as this larger, cosmic figure of rebellion, not merely a riverbank animal.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees the image casts Pharaoh's pride as something only God can net and slay; the wider dragon imagery simply deepens the connection to Scripture's ultimate defeat of the ancient serpent.

study.originalLanguages: Ezekiel is told to take up a qinah, a formal funeral lament, over a king who is still alive. Pharaoh is called a tanniyn, a sea monster or dragon, the same word Scripture uses elsewhere for the primeval chaos-creature Rahab or Leviathan, casting Egypt's pride as opposition to God's ordered world itself. Every fallen nation in the chapter's second half shares the disgrace of being arel, uncircumcised, in death.

typology.type: The chaos-monster Pharaoh, netted and slain, Ezekiel 32:2-4
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the final defeat of 'that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan', bound and later cast into the lake of fire, Revelation 12:9, 20:2, 10.

typology.type: The darkened sun, moon, and stars over Egypt's judgment, Ezekiel 32:7-8
typology.fulfillment: Echoes the plague of darkness at the Exodus, Exodus 10:21-23, and anticipates the darkness over Calvary when God's judgment fell on Christ in our place, Matthew 27:45.

study.christ: The darkened sun, moon, and stars over Egypt's judgment, verses 7-8, echo the plague of darkness at the Exodus, Exodus 10:21-23, and anticipate the darkness over Calvary, Matthew 27:45, where God's judgment on sin fell on His own Son rather than on the nations who deserved it. The chaos-dragon imagery used for Pharaoh here previews the ancient serpent's final undoing: Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities at the cross, making a public spectacle of them, Colossians 2:15, and will one day cast the dragon himself into the lake of fire forever, Revelation 20:10.
study.god: God alone commands the depths, the stars, and the nations; He nets the great sea monster as easily as a fisherman his catch, showing that no power, however primeval or terrifying in its pride, escapes His sovereign judgment. Yet He is also the God who lets Pharaoh find his only 'comfort' in discovering he is not alone in his disgrace, a solemn and sobering justice, not cruelty for its own sake.

crossReferences.note: Calls on the LORD's arm to awake as in the days of old, when it cut Rahab in pieces and pierced the dragon, the same chaos-monster imagery used of Egypt here.
The plague of darkness over Egypt at the Exodus is echoed in the darkened sun, moon, and stars over Pharaoh's downfall in this chapter.

The ancient serpent and dragon, identified as the devil and Satan, is finally bound and then cast into the lake of fire, the ultimate defeat previewed whenever Scripture nets and slays a chaos-monster king.
At the cross Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities and made a public spectacle of them, the decisive defeat behind every lesser judgment on proud, monstrous power.

study.oneStory: This is the last word against Egypt's pride: pictured as the primeval sea monster, Pharaoh is netted, his skies darkened, and his multitude consigned to Sheol among every other terror-spreading nation already there. It anticipates the far greater and final undoing of the ancient dragon himself, accomplished at the cross and consummated when he is cast forever into the lake of fire.

quiz.q: How does Ezekiel first picture Pharaoh in this lament, verse 2?

options.text: Like a lion among the nations and a monster in the seas, thrashing about and muddying the waters.
options.note: Verse 2 uses exactly this double image for Pharaoh's proud, destructive presence.

options.text: Like a fallen cedar tree, echoing the previous chapter's image.
options.note: The cedar image belongs to Ezekiel 31; this chapter's image is a lion and sea monster.

options.text: Like a shepherd who abandoned his flock.
options.note: Shepherd imagery for failed leaders belongs to Ezekiel 34, not this lament.

options.text: Like a watchman asleep at his post.
options.note: Watchman imagery belongs to Ezekiel 33, not this passage.

quiz.q: What happens to the heavens when God brings Pharaoh down, verses 7-8?

options.text: God darkens the sun, moon, and stars over Egypt.
options.note: Verses 7-8 state this darkening explicitly, echoing the Exodus plague of darkness.

options.text: A great earthquake shakes the land of Egypt.
options.note: No earthquake is mentioned here; the judgment is described through cosmic darkness.

options.text: Fire falls from heaven onto Egypt's cities.
options.note: The described judgment is darkness in the heavens, not fire falling.

options.text: Nothing unusual happens in the skies.
options.note: The opposite is true: the heavens are explicitly darkened over Egypt's downfall.

quiz.q: Whose sword does God name as the specific instrument of Egypt's judgment, verse 11?

options.text: The sword of the king of Babylon.
options.note: Verse 11 names Babylon's king directly as the coming instrument of judgment.

options.text: The sword of Assyria.
options.note: Assyria appears later as one of the already-fallen nations in Sheol, not as Egypt's coming judge here.

options.text: The sword of Edom.
options.note: Edom also appears later among the fallen nations, not as the instrument of judgment named in verse 11.

options.text: No specific nation is named as the instrument of judgment.
options.note: A specific nation, Babylon, is named directly in verse 11.

quiz.q: In the second lament, which nations does Ezekiel say are already lying in Sheol among the slain?

options.text: Assyria, Elam, Meshech and Tubal, Edom, and the princes of the north with the Sidonians.
options.note: Verses 22-30 name each of these nations in turn as already lying disgraced among the slain.

options.text: Only Assyria and Babylon.
options.note: The roll call names several more nations besides Assyria; Babylon is the instrument of judgment, not among the slain listed here.

options.text: Only the small nations bordering the Nile.
options.note: The nations named are major regional powers, not minor neighbors of Egypt.

options.text: No other nations are mentioned, only Egypt itself.
options.note: The entire point of verses 22-30 is that Egypt joins many other named nations already in Sheol.

quiz.q: What is Pharaoh's grim 'comfort' at the chapter's end, verse 31?

options.text: Seeing that he is not alone: all his multitude and army share the same fate as every other slain, terror-spreading nation.
options.note: Verse 31 states plainly that Pharaoh is 'comforted' only by this shared disgrace in death.

options.text: Learning that Egypt's throne will be restored after a short exile.
options.note: No such restoration is promised in this chapter; the tone throughout is unrelieved judgment.

options.text: Discovering that his descendants will avoid the same judgment.
options.note: The text speaks of Pharaoh and 'all his multitude' sharing the fate together, not an exemption for descendants.

options.text: Being spared from Sheol entirely because of his lament.
options.note: The opposite is true: verse 32 confirms Pharaoh himself will be laid to rest among the uncircumcised slain.

questions: Pharaoh is pictured as a great monster thrashing and muddying the waters around him, seemingly unaware of how exposed he is to God. Where might your own strength, busyness, or ambition be stirring up trouble you cannot quite see?

God darkens the sun, moon, and stars over Egypt's downfall, the same kind of judgment poured out at the Exodus and echoed again at the cross. How does knowing God has already judged sin fully at Calvary change how you face His discipline or correction now?

Pharaoh's only 'comfort' is finding he is not alone in the pit; there is no real comfort in shared disgrace. Where are you tempted to find comfort in the fact that others are struggling or failing just like you, rather than turning to Christ?

This chaos-monster imagery for Egypt's pride is ultimately defeated at the cross and finally at Christ's return, Revelation 20:10. How does the certainty of evil's final defeat give you courage for whatever 'monster' you are facing today?
The long roll call of fallen nations, Assyria, Elam, Edom, and more, shows that no empire's terror lasts forever. What are you trusting in today that this passage would call you to hold more loosely?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew word translated 'monster' (tannin) elsewhere in Scripture names the primeval chaos-dragon, Rahab or Leviathan; some read it here simply as Egypt's well-known Nile crocodile, while others hear the fuller, cosmic overtone of a power opposed to God's ordered world.

The BSB's 'uncircumcised' renders a term of covenant disgrace; applying it to Egypt and these other nations groups them together in shame regardless of their earthly might, a deliberate irony given how feared each once was.

The reference to warriors buried 'with their weapons of war... under their heads' reflects an ancient burial custom honoring soldiers; the text notes these particular nations are denied even that dignity in death.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg/Truth For Life, Keller/Gospel in Life, Piper/Desiring God, Ferguson, Carson/The Gospel Coalition) addressing Ezekiel 32 specifically was found via live web search this session, so sermon status is set to none rather than fabricated.
